paper.title: On the Role of Optimization in Double Descent: A Least Squares Study
paper.abstract: Empirically it has been observed that the performance of deep neural networks steadily improves with increased model size, contradicting the classical view on overfitting and generalization. Recently, the double descent phenomenon has been proposed to reconcile this observation with theory, suggesting that the test error has a second descent when the model becomes sufficiently overparameterized, as the model size itself acts as an implicit regularizer. In this paper we add to the growing body of work in this space, providing a careful study of learning dynamics as a function of model size for the least squares scenario. We show an excess risk bound for the gradient descent solution of the least squares objective. The bound depends on the smallest non-zero eigenvalue of the sample covariance matrix of the input features, via a functional form that has the double descent behaviour. This gives a new perspective on the double descent curves reported in the literature, as our analysis of the excess risk allows to decouple the effect of optimization and generalization error. In particular, we find that in the case of noiseless regression, double descent is explained solely by optimization-related quantities, which was missed in studies focusing on the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse solution. We believe that our derivation provides an alternative view compared to existing works, shedding some light on a possible cause of this phenomenon, at least in the considered least squares setting. We empirically explore if our predictions hold for neural networks, in particular whether the spectrum of the sample covariance of features at intermediary hidden layers has a similar behaviour as the one predicted by our derivations in the least squares setting.

review_fields.summary: The paper studies the phenomenon of double descent (DD), and how different parameters of the problem affect this phenomenon. First, the authors show several theoretical results on linear predictors in the over-parameterized regime, with and without label noise. These results indicate that a certain eigenvalue of the data matrix affects the generalization error. Next, several experiments are shown on the MNIST and FashionMNIST datasets, that there is a correlation between this eigenvalue and the DD phenomenon.
review_fields.main_review:
I think that DD is still not well understood, and studying it is important and interesting. Also, the connection between DD and certain parameters of the data or optimization is an interesting observation. On the other hand, I think this paper makes a weak case for why these parameters actually affect DD, especially on the experimental part, and vaguely connects the theory presented in the paper with the experiments. In more details:

For the theoretical part:
1) I think the main idea is nice, studying linear regression in the case where d>n (more parameters than data examples), which is a less studied case in the literature. 

2) Viewing section 2 as a whole, it is hard to grasp a single conclusion on how the excess risk behaves. It would have been much clearer if there was a single theorem that shows a bound on the excess risk when d goes to infinity. In both theorems 2 & 3, there are terms where it is not clear how they behave (e.g. E[||w^*||^2]_{I-M}).

3) To emphasize the previous point, two parameters are considered (n and d), and throughout the section it is unclear which one is considered to be larger. I assume the authors meant that d>n (since this is the over-parameterized case), but in line 163 (and Appendix F) it seems the E[||w^*||^2]_{I-M} term only goes to zero with large n, as well as the noise in Theorem 3. What happens in the over-parameterized regime when n is fixed and d goes to infinity? I think this is the interesting case when studying DD.

4) Section 3 is very unclear. What are the assumptions made in the equation after line 216? What are the main conclusions of this section? There are very long paragraphs, where the bottom line isn’t clear.

For the empirical part
1) I think this is the main weak point of the paper. The connection between the experiments and the theory is not well grounded. Also, the experiments are very lacking and not convincing enough.

2) To emphasize, the theoretical part focuses on a certain eigenvalue of the data matrix. This eigenvalue has no connection at all to the optimization process and is fixed. On the other hand, in the theoretical part the authors consider the eigenvalue of the output of some intermediary layer, which changes through the optimization process. Why not take the eigenvalue of the data itself? And if the authors already decide to take the eigenvalue of an intermediary layer, I suggest taking the eigenvalue of all layers and comparing them. 

3) The only datasets used are MNIST and FashionMNIST which I think are very small and not very hard to learn, even with simple models. To give a more convincing argument I suggest using larger and harder datasets (e.g. CIFAR 10/100, ImageNet).

4) The models considered are very small, e.g. one hidden layer and three hidden layers. I suggest experimenting on more realistic models and see if this eigenvalue has the same effect. Also, to experiment on other architectures such as ConvNets.

5) There are no experiments on regularization (e.g. L1/L2, Dropout, BatchNorm). Regularization techniques might mitigate the effect of this eigenvalue on the DD phenomenon. Without these experiments, it is hard to evaluate whether this phenomenon is due to the effects of this eigenvalue, or some other property that can be mitigated using regularization techniques.

6) In line 308 it says that ablation studies are in Appendix H. The only experiment in Appendix H is a three-layer network with or without skip connections. I suggest adding significantly more ablation studies to give a more convincing case.

Some more issues:

1) Why do the authors show the experiments for a different number of iterations? The reason is not explained in the paper, this is very confusing and takes three times as much space. I think the point of DD is looking at a trained model, if the authors want to emphasize some idea for an untrained model (e.g. after T=1000 iterations) then they should explain it.

2) There are confusing notational issues throughout the paper: the parameters alpha and T are shown in the introduction, but are defined only in section 2. Some notations are used in the main part of the paper, but are only defined in the appendix (e.g. ||A||_M norm). It is OK to define some notations in the appendix, but if something appears in the main part of the paper, then I think it should be defined there.

3) Line 131, what does it mean that \Delta and W_0 are independent? Each coordinate of W_0 is i.i.d Gaussian, so how can it be dependent on \Delta. Is it meant to be that \Delta is independent of d?

4) In all the theorems, why is W_0 upper case? This is a vector, and the notations state that vectors are lower case, also w and w^* are lower case.

5) The figures are extremely small and hard to read. I suggest the authors to remove the different number of iterations experiments and just show how the trained model behaves, this will save much space and will allow to give much bigger and clearer figures.

6) In lines 699-700 it states that “the deeper the network the harder it is to train them”, while Figure 6 shows the opposite, that deeper networks achieve smaller test error. 

7) The title is “On the Role of Optimization in Double Descent..”, while the paper studies how a certain eigenvalue of the data matrix affects DD. 

8) The "related work" section is in the appendix. Although this is allowed, in my opinion, this is bad practice. This section is a crucial part of the paper and helps the reader to evaluate its contribution and novelty, based on previous works that were done in the same area.

Some typos and other minor issues:

1) Sometimes “optimization” is used and other times “optimisation”. Both are OK, but the authors should be consistent and choose one form.

2) Title of Section 3: parammeterization → parameterization

3) In figure 1, “The first row demonstrates behavior of (1)...” what is (1)? The hyperlink points to the introduction section.

4) First sentence of appendix F, should it be the over-parameterized case?


To improve the paper I suggest making a more thorough experimental study. This includes more realistic datasets, different architectures, Verify whether the conclusions hold with different regularization schemes, and conduct more ablation studies. Also, to connect the empirical results to the theoretical study I suggest looking at different architectures and network depths, and calculate the eigenvalue for more intermediary layers (including for the data itself). It will also help if the theoretical part will have a single corollary or theorem on how the excess risk behaves when d->\infty, without any unclear terms such as E[||w^*||^2]_{I-M}.

-------------Update after rebuttal---------------

After reading the author's response my concerns remain, and I retain my score.
review_fields.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work
